learn.title: Sodium-ion Batteries Affordable Alternative
learn.intro: Sodium-ion batteries use abundant sodium instead of scarce lithium, cutting material costs and supply risk. They enable cheaper storage for solar and wind, supporting grid stability. With lithium prices volatile, sodium-ion offers a resilient path to decarbonize energy systems affordably across diverse geographies.
learn.c1_text: Sodium is found in seawater and salt; lithium is rarer. Like swapping gold for sand, sodium cells avoid scarce mining. Sodium costs about $0.2/kg vs lithium $15/kg, making large batteries 20-30% cheaper per kWh to build.
learn.c2_text: Sodium-ion has lower energy density (~120 Wh/kg vs 250 for lithium) but works in -20°C. Think of a heavier backpack with same daily use. They suit stationary grid storage where weight matters less than cost and safety.
learn.c3_text: India's Reliance plans sodium-ion gigafactories by 2025 under PLI scheme. With 70% lithium imported, sodium reduces dependency. Pilot plants in Gujarat target 10 GWh annual capacity, boosting local clean storage manufacturing.
learn.stat1: Sodium abundance: 2.3% of Earth's crust vs lithium 0.002%
learn.stat2: Sodium-ion cell cost ~30% lower than LFP lithium

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage World Battery
learn.intro: Pumped hydro stores energy by moving water uphill when power is cheap, releasing it to generate electricity on demand. It provides 90% of global storage capacity, balancing variable renewables. This giant 'water battery' is vital for reliable net-zero grids.
learn.c1_text: Two reservoirs at different heights act like a rechargeable dam. Excess solar pumps water up; demand peaks release it through turbines. A 1 GW plant lifting 100m stores 8 GWh, enough for 8 hours of city power.
learn.c2_text: Pumped hydro is 70-85% efficient, losing some energy as heat. Like a bicycle uphill then down, most effort returns. It offers seconds-fast response, outperforming chemical batteries for multi-hour grid balancing at scale.
learn.c3_text: India has 4.7 GW pumped storage, targeting 50 GW by 2030 under NEP. Tehri stage-2 (1000 MW) aids solar integration. PSPs support 500 GW non-fossil capacity goal, reducing coal peaker reliance in evening ramps.
learn.stat1: Pumped hydro = 90% of world's storage capacity

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Next Gen
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells promise efficiencies above 33% vs silicon's 26%, using cheap printable materials. They could slash panel costs and enable flexible rooftops. NREL research pushes stability, key to replacing fossil generation with ubiquitous, high-yield photovoltaics.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites absorb wide light spectrum like a sponge vs silicon strainer. Lab cells hit 26% single-junction, 33% tandem with silicon. A 1 m2 perovskite yields 330 W vs 260 W silicon, boosting output per roof area 25%.
learn.c2_text: Moisture degrades perovskites faster than silicon's 25-year life. Encapsulation adds glass like phone screen protector. NREL achieved 1-year stable operation; commercial goal is 20-year life to match silicon bankability for utilities.
learn.c3_text: IISc Bangalore builds tandem perovskite-silicon achieving 28% in lab. National Solar Mission funds 2 MW demo in Rajasthan by 2025. With 90 GW solar, perovskites can cut land use 20% for same generation.

learn.title: Capacity Factor Explained
learn.intro: Capacity factor is actual output divided by max possible, revealing real renewable performance. It prevents overestimating wind or solar contribution, guiding grid planning. Understanding it helps policymakers size storage and avoid blackouts during low-output periods.
learn.c1_text: A 100 MW solar farm at 20% capacity factor yields 20 MW average, like a car averaging 20 km/h in traffic vs 100 top speed. It = actual generation / (rated MW × hours). Explains why 1 GW nameplate ≠ constant 1 GW.
learn.c2_text: US solar CF ~24%, wind ~35%, nuclear ~90% due to fuel continuity. Think of intermittent rain vs steady river. Planners use CF to calculate needed panels: 1 GW solar needs 4 GW build for 1 GW average.
learn.c3_text: India solar CF 19-22% (high irradiance Rajasthan 22%), wind 25-30% Tamil Nadu. CEA data shows 50 GW solar generated 110 BU in 2023, implying 25% CF. Helps DISCOMs schedule coal backup accurately.

learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifespan Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines last 20-25 years; blades are tough fiberglass waste. Recycling avoids landfill and recovers materials, crucial as early farms retire. IRENA guides circular strategies, ensuring wind's clean label holds through full lifecycle and cost declines.
learn.c1_text: Turbines run 20-25 years with bearing swaps every 5 yrs. Like car servicing, oil checks prevent failure. A 3 MW turbine blade is 50m; 85% of mass is recyclable steel, only blades pose challenge at end-of-life.
learn.c2_text: Blades use glass fiber in epoxy, hard to separate. Co-processing in cement kilns replaces coal, saving 1 ton CO2 per ton blade. Mechanical shredding recovers 90% filler; pyrolysis emerging to reclaim fibers for auto parts.
learn.c3_text: India has 45 GW wind; 5 GW to retire by 2030. Suzlon recycles blades via cement co-processing in Maharashtra. MNRE draft policy mandates 85% reuse. Tamil Nadu plant diverts 10,000 tons blades yearly from landfill.

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels
learn.intro: Bifacial panels capture sunlight from front and reflected ground light, boosting yield 5-20% without extra land. They accelerate solar ROI and support dense deployments. IRENA highlights them as cost-effective for utility-scale clean energy expansion worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial cells sit on transparent backsheet, like sunglasses absorbing rear glare. White gravel boosts albedo; a 1 MW plant gains 100-200 kW extra vs monofacial. Yield rises 10% in desert, 5% on grass, free from backside sun.
learn.c2_text: They require elevated racks and reflective ground (snow/soil) for rear light. Think of mirror under lamp doubling room brightness. Trackers add 15% bifacial gain; cleaning rear occasionally maintains 95% performance vs soiling loss.
learn.c3_text: Bhadla solar park (2.2 GW Rajasthan) uses bifacial with trackers, gaining 12% output. SECI tenders mandate bifacial for 30 GW by 2025. With 90 GW solar, shift saves 18 GW land-equivalent capacity nationally.
